coredata.dc:title: Subcellular fluorescence imaging for BHK cell and multiple sensing based on carbon dots with two strong emission peaks
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Bo
Gong, Deyan
Li, Xinran
Zhang, Lilan
Dong, Yaping
Li, Wu
Liu, Weisheng
Qin, Wenwu
Wang, Yabin
coredata.dc:description: We develop a novel kind of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with unprecedented properties through hydrothermal treatment of soluble starch and ethylenediamine. Such N-CDs can emit two strong emission peaks at about 450nm and 540nm under a single-wavelength ultraviolet-light excitation. The intensity ratios of the two emission peaks are influenced by pH values, leading to the change of fluorescent colours, which can be utilized to detect solution pH by the change of fluorescent colours. Most importantly, N-CDs can act as fluorescence probes for labelling cell nucleus of BHK cells selectively, indicating that the as-prepared N-CDs have specific targeting towards nucleus cells. In addition, the fluorescence intensity can be quenched by Fe3+ ion and restored by ascorbic acid effectively. The detection limits for Fe3+ and AA were 84.9nM and 62.1nM, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Subcellular
Fluorescence imaging
on-off-on
Multiple sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell culture, cytotoxicity assay and fluorescence imaging
Detection of Fe3+ and AA with PL “on-off-on”
Optical properties of N-CDs5000 and the dependence on pH
Formation and structural features of CD
“On-off-on” strategy for sensing Fe3+ and AA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) UV–vis absorption spectra (black) and photoluminescence emission spectra (green) excited at 360nm. The corresponding photoluminescence excitation spectra of N-CDs5000 aqueous solution at 450nm (red) and 540nm (blue) under alkaline condition (pH=10). The insets are photographs of N-CDs5000 aqueous solution under visible light (left) and 365nm UV (right). B) Photoluminescence emission spectra of N-CDs5000 aqueous solution at pH=10 excitated from 310nm to 380nm. C) The changes of SEP and LEP with excitation wavelength. D) Decay curves of N-CDs5000 at 450nm and 540nm under alkaline condition. E) and F) Photoluminescence emission spectra excited at 360nm of N-CDs5000 aqueous solution at various pH values. Insets in Fig. F are photographs of N-CDs5000 aqueous solution under 365nm UV light. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
TEM images of O-CDs A), N-CDs1000 B), N-CDs3000 C), and N-CDs5000 D), respectively. The insets are the according size distributions with average diameters 3.67, 1.63, 1.53 and 1.19nm, respectively.
A) FT-IR spectra and B) XPS survey scan spectra of (a) O-CDs, (b) N-CDs1000, (c) N-CDs3000, and (d) N-CDs5000.

Confocal fluorescence images of BHK cells incubated with 200μg/mL of N-CDs5000 for 2h. A) the bright-field image, B), C), and D) were fluorographies excitated at 405nm, as well as recorded at 420–510nm, 490–560nm, and 590–750nm, respectively.
The various fluorescence intensity of N-CDs5000 solution with various individual metal ions (500μM) at pH 3.

A) The fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs5000 in the presence of Fe3+ along with concentration increased (0–1000μM) at pH 3. Insets are photographs ofN-CDs5000 aqueous solution at Fe3+ concentration of 0 (left) and 1000μM (right) under 365nm UV light. B) The relationship of fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ (0–1000μM). Inset is the quenching plot of N-CDs5000 at Fe3+ concentration of 0–80μM. C) The fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs5000 with 200μM Fe3+ and various AA concentrations (0–150μM) at pH 3. Insets are photographs of N-CDs5000 aqueous solution with 200μM Fe3+ and 0 (left) as well as 150μM (right) AA under 365nm UV light. D) The relationship between fluorescence intensity and AA. Inset is the enhancing plot of N-CDs5000 with 200μM Fe3+ at AA concentration of 0–100μM. E) Schematic illustrations of synthesis route of N-CDs5000, and “on-off-on” detection mechanism for Fe3+ and AA.
Fluorescence probes of CDs for Fe3+ and AA based on redox mechanism.

head.ce:title: Subcellular fluorescence imaging for BHK cell and multiple sensing based on carbon dots with two strong emission peaks

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qinghai Institute of Salt Lakes, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Xining, 810008, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Qinghai Institute of Salt Lakes, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Xining, 810008, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel kind of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was developed with unprecedented properties, which can emit two strong emission peaks at about 450nm and 540nm under a single-wavelength ultraviolet-light excitation, label nucleus of BHK cells, and detect Fe3+ ion and ascorbic acid as “on-off-on” probe.

We develop a novel kind of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with unprecedented properties through hydrothermal treatment of soluble starch and ethylenediamine. Such N-CDs can emit two strong emission peaks at about 450nm and 540nm under a single-wavelength ultraviolet-light excitation. The intensity ratios of the two emission peaks are influenced by pH values, leading to the change of fluorescent colours, which can be utilized to detect solution pH by the change of fluorescent colours. Most importantly, N-CDs can act as fluorescence probes for labelling cell nucleus of BHK cells selectively, indicating that the as-prepared N-CDs have specific targeting towards nucleus cells. In addition, the fluorescence intensity can be quenched by Fe3+ ion and restored by ascorbic acid effectively. The detection limits for Fe3+ and AA were 84.9nM and 62.1nM, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, much attention has been paid to photoluminescence (PL) probes for detecting pH, metal ions and biological molecules in ecological and biological fields, owing to their merits of high specificity, sensitivity, and real-time determination [1–7]. Carbon dots (CDs) as a new carbon-based fluorescence nano structures have been attracting lots of attention due to their outstanding optical properties since its discovery in 2004 [8]. Various raw materials and different approaches have been utilized to synthesize CDs [9–12]. Unlike other fluorescent materials, semiconductor quantum dots [13,14], organic dyes [15,16] and rare earth metal nano-particles [17–19], CDs are advantageous both in their green synthesis methods and unique features, such as high photostability, easy functionalization, good aqueous solubility, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [20–25]. These excellent capabilities also render CDs promising candidates as novel and sensitive sensors in bio-imaging, fluorescence sensors, drug delivery, optoelectronic devices, etc. [26–28].

PL probes of CDs have been rapidly developed in the last few years, aiming at detecting substances and quantities by measuring the increase or decrease from fluorescence intensity (turn-on or −off) [29–34]. CDs surfaces can be tuned and functionalized by chemical modification, which endows CDs with molecular recognition to detect specific ions and biological molecules more flexibly based on“on-off-on” detection mechanism. For example, Qu et al. explored a kind of specific CDs and utilized them as effective fluorescent probes for label-free detection of iron (III) ion and dopamine. The fluorescence from the as-prepared CDs was significantly quenched by hydroquinone groups which were generated by oxidation reaction between quinone species and iron (III) ion. The quenched fluorescent could be immediately recovered through the addition of dopamine [35]. Han’s group developed a N-doped CDs (N-CDs) to detect iron (III) ion and apoferritin by the sensitive and selective fluorescent ability. Iron (III) could form complexes by coordination reaction on N-CDs surface, which quenches the fluorescence effectively. The fluorescence was reversibly restored by adding apoferritin on the basis of competition mechanism [36]. Xie et al. also synthesized ionic liquid-functionalized CDs as strong fluorescence quenching platform for responding to Iron (III). Fluorescent could be obviously restored via a redox mechanism [37].

CDs can likewise be adopted for pH determination except for metal ions and biological molecules. As is known, pH plays pivotal roles in various physiological processes and the abnormal pH change may lead us to diagnose the disease accurately [38,39]. Monitoring pH precisely and conveniently is of importance to environment and life safety. Numerous CDs-based fluorescent probes have been widely exploited and employed to test pH [40,41]. In order to further improve the accuracy and reliability of CDs-based fluorescent probes, ratiometric pH probes have been developed which were modified by embedding different fluorescent groups like dyes [42,43]. However, the as-designed probes and the preparation courses have some drawbacks, such as dual excitation mode, tedious procedures and complex separation. What is more, there are few label-free CDs with pH-sensitive ratiometric ability under a single-wavelength excitation. Qu et al. reported a CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent nano-sensor and utilized to detect pH value ranging from 6.0 to 8.6 under a 380nm excitation [44]. All in all, it is very urgent to produce CDs with multiple fluorescence emission under a single excitation for ratiometric measurement.

Simultaneously, CDs were extensively utilized as cell fluorescent imaging as a result of their excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity and small sizes [26,45]. It is noteworthy that most CDs can locate in the cytoplasm but be difficult to get into nucleus. Specific structure of nucleus accounts for this phenomenon. Datta et al. recently constructed a hybrid type of CDs with anionic sheets via non-covalent interactions [46], and the as-prepared fluorescent materials can label cytoplasm or cell nucleus selectively by controlling the relative amount of CDs on graphene oxide. Yang’s group developed CDs with polyethylene glycol, functionalized them by signal peptide, and employed the obtained CDs as cell nucleus imaging material [47]. Although great efforts have been made to design original probes for nucleus imaging, CDs probes that can selectively stain nucleus are scarce and needed.

Herein, we develop a simple and low-cost synthesis strategy to fabricate CDs doped with nitrogen (N-CDs) by hydrothermal treatment of soluble starch (SS) and ethylenediamine (EDA). As the amount of SS is 0.162g and EDA is 5000μL (marked as N-CDs5000), the as-prepared products emit two strong emission peaks at about 450nm and 540nm under a single-wavelength ultraviolet-light excitation. The intensity ratios of the two emission peaks are influenced by pH values, leading to the change of fluorescent colours. Surprisingly, N-CDs5000 with low cytotoxicity can serve as very effective fluorescence probes for fluorescence imaging of cellular nucleus for BHK cell. To the best of our knowledge, the aforementioned properties are novel and seldom reported. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs5000 can be effectively quenched by Fe3+ ion and restored by ascorbic acid (AA). The detection limits for Fe3+ and AA were 84.9nM and 62.1nM, respectively. Therefore, N-CDs5000 can be a promising “on-off-on” probe for Fe3+ and AA.

Soluble starch (SS) and ascorbic acid (AA) were purchased from Guangfu Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). Ethylenediamine (EDA, 99%) was obtained from Sinopharm chemical Regent Co. Ltd (Beijing, China).The Li+, Na+, K+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+,Ni2+and Cu2+ chlorides, Zn2+ acetate, Fe2+ sulfate and Cd2+, Pb2+, Hg2+ and Ag+ nitrates were purchased from heowns (Tianjing, China). All reagents are of analytical grade. The water used in all the experiments was ultrapure water self-made from a Milli-Qultrapure (18.2MΩcm) system.

X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) equipped with an Mg Kα (hν =1253.6eV) were acquired on a PHI 550 photoelectron spectrometer. X-ray gun operated at 20mA and 15kV. The background pressure of residual gases was lower than 10−6 Pa during the measurements. XRD measurements with Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.54059Å) were performed on a X-ray diffractometer (Philips X’Pert, Holland). Operating current and voltage were set at 35mA and 40kV, respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured using a Nicolet 360 FTIR spectrometer with the KBr pellet technique. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was taken on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope at a 120kV acceleration voltage. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained on an Agilent UV Cary100 spectrophotometer. FLS920 spectrofluorometer was used to measure fluorescence lifetimes, quantum yields, and steady-state emission spectra. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured with the light emitting diodes at a 360nm excitation wavelength on an Edinburgh Instruments FLS920.

In a typical synthesis route, 0.162g of soluble starch and different amount of EDA 0μL, 1000μL, 3000μL, 5000μL were dissolved in 10mL deionized water. The precursor solution was transferred to a 20mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated to 200°C and maintained for 5h after sealing. The reactors were cooled to room temperature naturally. Through centrifugal processing (10000 r/min, 5min), the yellow transparent upper solution was obtained. The obtained solution was dried to remove exceed EDA molecules and water by means of rotary evaporator at 90°C. The samples were then dissolved in 10mL deionized water and dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (MWCO 1000Da from Sigma-Aldrich) to further remove the excess reagents for 24h. The as-obtained samples were dried by vacuum freeze drying finally. The products were denoted as O-CDs, N-CDs1000, N-CDs3000 and N-CDs5000 with EDA of 0μL, 1000μL, 3000μL and 5000μL, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture, cytotoxicity assay and fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Baby Hamster Syrian Kindey (BHK) cells supplemented with 10% FBS (fetal bovine serum) were cultured in DMEM (Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium). Cellular fluorescent images were recorded by an Olympus FV1000-IX81 laser confocal microscope. The cell lines were maintained under a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2 at 37°C. Cells were incubated in a 1.0mL fresh culture medium with 200μg/mL N-CDs5000 for 2h after removal of culture medium. The obtained cells were washed three times using PBS to remove the residual nano-particles before imaging measurement.

BHK cells were seeded in 96-well assay plates at a density of 104 cells per well for 24h (100μL total volume/well). Afterwards, N-CDs5000 were added to cell culture medium at indicated concentrations of 20, 40, 80, 160, 200μg/mL which were incubated for 24h. In order to determine toxicity, 3-(4, 5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-5-(3-carboxymethoxyphenyl)-2-(4-sulfophenyl)-2H-tetrazolium and inner salts (MTS) was added to each micro-titer plate well, incubating in the CO2 incubator for another 24h. Absorbance values were gained with Bio-Rad model-680 micro-plate reader at 450nm. Cell viability was estimated in accordance with the following equation: cell viability (%)=mean absorbance value of treatment group/mean absorbance value of control×100%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ and AA with PL “on-off-on”
ce:section.ce:para: Detecting Fe3+ was carried out in aqueous solution of N-CDs5000 at pH 3 under room temperature. Fe3+ solutions with serial concentrations were added into the CDs aqueous solution to evaluate the sensitivity. As for a typical assay, 1mL Fe3+ solution was mixed with 1mL N-CDs5000 solution. PL spectra were performed by operating the fluorescence spectrometer with an 360nm excitation wavelength. Reference sample was prepared by mixing 1mL pure water with 1mL N-CDs5000 solution. Similar procedures were conducted to the detect other metal ions (Ag+,Al3+, Ba2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Hg2+,Cu2+, Fe2+, Li+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, and Zn2+) with a 1mM concentration.

AA detection was proceeded in N-CDs5000 aqueous solutions with 1mM Fe3+ at pH 3. Standard AA stock solutions were prepared by dissolving appropriate amount of AA in ultrapure water. To evaluate the sensitivity of the as-prepared samples towards AA, serial concentrations of AA solution were added into N-CDs5000 aqueous solution with 1mM Fe3+. In a typical assay, 1mL AA solution was mixed with 1mL N-CDs5000 solution. PL spectra were also performed as described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of N-CDs5000 and the dependence on pH
ce:section.ce:para: SS and EDA as carbon and nitrogen sources were applied to develop N-CDs with various functional groups. UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of the as-prepared N-CDs5000 were investigated. Fig. 1 A shows that two characteristic peaks of UV–vis absorption are located at about 285nm and 335nm, respectively. The former can be assigned to π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains from the carbon core typically. The latter with a broader absorption shoulder can be ascribed to the n-π* transition from surface moieties [36,48]. When N-CDs5000 was excitated by 360nm wavelength, the corresponding PL spectra exhibit two peaks at 449nm (short emission peak) and 543nm (long emission peak) with stoke shifts of 89nm and 183nm, respectively (Fig. 1A). The color of N-CDs5000 diluted aqueous solution is yellow under 365nm UV light excitation (the inset in Fig. 1A).

The PL spectra of N-CDs5000 display excitation-dependent features for short emission peaks (SEP) and excitation-independent features for long emission peaks (LEP), demonstrated in Fig. 1B and C. When excitated from 310nm to 380nm, SEP remarkably shift towards longer wavelength region. For LEP excitated under the same wavelength, no shift was observed and the peaks maintain at about 544nm. The wavelength gap between SEP and LEP decreases from 141.8nm to 96.5nm. The intensity ratio (SEP/LEP) first reduces and then increases (1.51–1.02–1.18 in Fig. 1C). Although PL properties of CDs in excitation-dependent or −independent have not been verified, it is generally believed that such properties are related to energy states that originate from carbon cores and surface structures, or caused by band gap transitions of conjugated π-domains [49–51].

Oxygen-containing groups (carbonyl, carboxyl and hydroxyl as examples) existed on CDs surface can introduce luminescence centers with different energy levels, leading to excitation-dependent PL properties [20,52]. Some other CDs passivated by doping other elements like nitrogen and sulfur exhibit excitation-independent emissions features, owing to uniform energy levels of luminescence centers in the carbon core and on their surface [49,53,54]. Fig. 1D shows the decay curves of N-CDs5000 at 450nm and 540nm excited with 360nm wavelength. Table S1 gives the fitted fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs5000.

The decay curve of N-CDs5000 at 450nm can be fitted with a triple-exponential decay and the average fluorescence lifetime is 8.26ns. At 540nm, the lifetime of N-CDs5000 is 4.85ns with a double-exponential decay. Additionally, N-CDs5000 possess excellent acid-evoked fluorescence enhancement ability (Fig. 1E). PL intensity increases linearly as pH value ranges from 11 to 3 at about 450nm (Fig. 1F). Relative intensity of the two emission peaks at about 450nm and 540nm varies, and the emission peak at 540nm have a blue shift. Above factors alter the color of N-CDs5000 solutions under 365nm UV light excitation at different pH value (the inset in Fig. 1F). All these results indicate that emissions at 450nm and 540nm maybe derive from different types of PL centers. Thus, another three kinds of CDs were prepared with fixed amount of SS (0.162g) and different amount of EDA (0, 1000μL, 3000μL), denoted as O-CDs, N-CDs1000, and N-CDs3000, respectively. Their structural features and optical properties were investigated as follows.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation and structural features of CD
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy images (TEM) of O-CDs, N-CDs1000,N-CDs3000, and N-CDs5000 are displayed in Fig. 2 . The as-prepared CDs are nearly spherical and own uniform particle size. The average diameter decreases from 3.67nm to 1.19nm with enhancement of EDA (the insets of Fig. 2). Similar results were acquired in previous studies and smaller clusters representative of amine groups endow CDs surfaces with higher stabilization [50,55]. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the as-prepared CDs exhibit typical broad diffraction peaks at about 20°, associated with graphitic structures (Fig. S1) [12].

FT-IR spectra of the as-prepared CDs were compared to identify the functional groups in Fig. 3 A. Abundant oxygen-containing groups (COOH, OH) exist on OCDs surface, such as −OH (3100∼3500cm−1), carboxylic CO (1743cm−1), and CO (1163cm−1) [56,57]. N-doped CDs are quite different from OCDs. Peaks at 3200–3400cm−1 and 1578cm−1 can be assigned to the NH groups. Peaks at 1326cm−1and 1647cm−1 can be attributed to CN and CO stretching vibrations, respectively [51]. Relative intensities of peaks about N-containing groups gradually increase as amount of EDA boosts, indicating that more amino-groups cover NCDs surfaces. C, N, and O can be detected in nitrogen-containing CDs; N cannot be detected in OCDs as demonstrated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Fig. 3B). Oxygen proportion decreases as nitrogen increases from OCDs to NCDs5000 as listed in Table S2. C1s high-resolution spectra of OCDs can be deconvoluted into three peaks (Fig. S2A), CC/CC (284.6eV), COH (286.1eV), and CO (287.8eV). As shown in Fig. S2B, O1s band contains three peaks at CO (531.7eV), CO (532.6eV), and OCO* (533.3eV). For N-doped CDs (Figs. S3–S5), C1s bands can be deconvoluted into three peaks, CC/CC (284.6eV), CN (285.5eV), and OCO/CN (287.3eV). N1s bands around 398.5eV, 399.4eV and 400.2eV can be assigned to pyridinic N, amino N, and pyrrolic N, respectively. O1s band can be decomposed into peaks at 531.2eV (CO) and 532.0eV (CO) [25]. XPS outcomes consist with that of the FT-IR.

Compared with OCDs, UV–vis absorptions improve and redshifts appear in N-doped CDs due to the auxochromic effect of electron donating groups (−NH2, −NHR, etc.) [58], as shown in Fig. S6. OCDs and NCDs have main emission peaks at about 450nm and excitation-dependent PL properties gradually weaken (Fig. S7). More importantly, emission peaks at about 540nm shows up in NCDs and become more obvious as EDA content increases, while OCDs does not possess this novel nature. As a result, N-related PL centers lead to an excitation-independent emission peak at about 540nm in NCDs.

In order to explore the effects of SS and EDA on fluorescence properties during the carbonization process, comparative trial were conducted with EDA as control factor solely. N-doped CDs (NCDsEDA) were obtained along with blue light emission, and their UV–vis absorption and PL spectra were shown in Fig. S8. In short, PL properties of NCDsEDA are apparently different from OCDs and N-doped CDs (NCDs1000, NCDs3000, and NCDs5000). Further, PL quantum yields (PLQY) of the as-prepared CDs were calculated by a comparative method using quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 solution as a standard sample and the result was listed in Table S3. NCDs1000, NCDs3000, and NCDs5000 possess excellent acid-evoked fluorescence enhancement abilities and show higher PLQY values at low pH value. On the contrary, both OCDs and NCDsEDA have higher PLQY at pH 7. Architectural feature, chemical composition and consequent optical properties may be responsible for this discrepancy as illustrated above. As for forming process and fabrication mechanism, polymer-like CDs emerge initially and then CDs with high content nitrogen develop through carbonization via a down-to-up synthetic route [56]. It can be concluded that both starch and EDA are involved in polymerization and carbonization processes.

To assess the prospects of NCDs5000 as a bioimaging material, standard 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-5-(3-carboxy-methoxy-phenyl)-2-(4-sulfophenyl)-2H-tetrazolium, inner salts (MTS) assays were performed using Baby Hamster Syrian Kindey (BHK) cells firstly [59]. NCDs5000 is nearly nontoxic at concentration of 200μg/mL (Fig. S9), similar to other CDs [60,61]. The low cytotoxicity reveals that NCDs5000 are suitable for bioimaging applications. Then, cell imaging capability of NCDs5000 was investigated by using a confocal microscope with BHK cells in vitro. Fig. 4 demonstrates the cell imaging results after incubating BHK cells loaded with NCDs5000 for 2h at 37°C. BHK cells emit strong blue, green, and red fluorescence under illumination at a single wavelength of 405nm (Fig. 4B–D). Almost of fluorescences were separately observed in the nuclear membrane and nuclear substance areas with apparent characteristics. NCDs5000 can specifically stain nucleus, indicating that NCDs5000 have specific targeting towards nucleus of BHK cells. Up to now, few non-functionalized CDs were created for fluorescence imaging of cellular nucleus. Main reasons are as follows: CDs must overcome multiple barriers against distinct and specific nucleus structures so as to enter cellular nucleus. In order to successfully permeate, CDs size should be less with a diameter of 8nm (a general diameter of a cell nucleus). Possible interactions of CDs with nucleic acids and chromatin should also be demanded in this situation [62,63]. Such specific targeting capability of staining nucleus is more advantageous than those of other carbon-based labelling agents [64]. Imaging experiments of swine kidney tubular epithelial cells (PK15) and HeLa human cervical cancer cells were performed under the same conditions further, to discuss the feasibility and flexibility of the as-prepared material as shown in Figs. S10 and S11. As a whole, NCDs5000 cannot express selective staining nucleus ability for PK15 and HeLa cells. NCDs5000 can effectively pass through the nuclear pore complex and enter the nucleus of PK15 cells. However, the presence both at the cytoplasm and in the nucleus indicates lack of selectivity. For HeLa cells, they only locate in the cytoplasm and cannot be found in nucleus. These attractive phenomena manifest that the selective nucleus imaging for NCDs5000 in BHK cells maybe results from not only their proper size distribution but also some specific interactions between surface groups and nucleic acids as well as chromatin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: “On-off-on” strategy for sensing Fe3+ and AA
ce:section.ce:para: Due to strong PL intensity and chemical stability in acidic conditions (Fig. 1E), N-CDs5000 was adopted for detecting metal ions under one acidic solution (pH=3). To evaluate the sensing ability of N-CDs5000 for different metal ions, fluorescence intensity was measured in the absent/present of metal ions, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 , it is clear that fluorescence intensity of N-CDs5000 can be effectively quenched by Fe3+ compared to blank with a 95.5% decrease. There is a 54.7% decrease for Hg2+ and about 20% decrease for Ag+, Co2+, Cr3+, respectively. Other metal ions (Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Cu2+,Na+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, and Zn2+) nearly maintain unchanged under the same conditions. The affinity between metal ions and specific functional groups on N-CDDs5000 surfaces may determine fluorescence quenching. Pyrrolic nitrogen can more strongly interact with Fe3+ than other metal ions [65].

As is confirmed that N-CDs5000 exhibit particular selectivity to Fe3+ ion, therefore, Fe3+ ion with different concentrations were added to N-CDs5000 aqueous solution and the fluorescence intensities were measured to investigate the sensitivity (Fig. 6 A). The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs5000 at around 450nm gradually decreases with increase of Fe3+ concentration. Emission peaks do not shift and no new band emerges. N-CDs5000 solution exhibits strong blue color without Fe3+, and the fluorescence is completely quenched when 1000μM of Fe3+ was added (the inset in Fig. 6A). At low Fe3+ concentrations from 0 to 80μM, fluorescence quenching follows a typical Stern-volmer equation with excellent linear correlation (F0/F=1+Ksv[Fe3+], Ksv=1.13×104 M−1, R2 =99.9%), as shown in Fig. 6B. The limit of detection (LOD) is 84.9nM calculated according to the IUPAC 3σ criterion.

It is worth noting that the fluorescence of N-CDs5000 can be effectively quenched by Fe3+, but no obvious change was observed with addition of Fe2+ at the same condition (Fig. 5). Considering that AA can reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+, thus, a conclusion can be drawn that any fluorescence changes observed in the presence of AA must be the results of specific interactions between AA and Fe3+. Fig. 6C displays the PL spectra of N-CDs5000 solution with 200μM Fe3+ and different concentrations of AA. The fluorescence intensity returns to about 70% and strong blue color emits again (the insets) when 150μM AA was added, compared to that of blank N-CDs5000. AA was also added to N-CDs5000 solution without Fe3+ as reference test, and the fluorescence is only slightly quenched as shown in Fig. S12, revealing that the recovery of fluorescence must be chemical reactions between AA and Fe3+. Fig. 6D presents the relationship of PL intensities versus AA concentration. At low concentrations from 0 to 100μM, fluorescence enhancement follows a typical Stern-volmer equation, possessing excellent linear correlation (F0/F=1+Ksv[AA], Ksv=1.4×104 M−1, R2 =99.9%) with LOD of 62.1nM.

To further identify the interaction, UV–vis absorption spectrum was carried out (Fig. S13). Compared to blank N-CDs5000 with typical absorption peaks at 285nm and 335nm, N-CDs5000 mixed with Fe3+ barely generates a new peak at 400nm. This result indicates that Fe3+ has high affinity to organic groups on N-CDs5000 surface yielding stable non-fluorescent complexes. Such complexes facilitate electron migration or energy transfer. When AA was introduced into N-CDs5000-Fe3+ system, the UV–vis absorption spectrum restores. The as-obtained stable complexes without fluorescene are destroyed because of Fe3+ to Fe2+ by AA. In order to further confirm the redox mechanism of fluorescence recovery, GHS was used to reduce Fe3+ instead of AA. Fluorescence intensity returns and strong blue color emits again as 150μM GHS was added (Fig. S14). Based on the aforementioned phenomenon, an “on-off-on” approach for detection of Fe3+ and AA are performed by using N-CDs5000 as fluorescent probes (Fig. 6E). Recent “on-off-on” PL probes applied to detect of Fe3+ and AA were summarized in Table 1 as a contrast.

In summary, we develop a simple and low-cost strategy to fabricate N-doped CDs by hydrothermal treatment of soluble starch (SS) and Ethylenediamine (EDA). The as-prepared N-CDs5000 emit two strong emission peaks at about 450nm and 540nm under a single-wavelength ultraviolet-light excitation. And emission peaks at about 540nm become more obvious as EDA content increases, while O-CDs does not possess this novel nature. The result indicates that N-related PL centers originated from EDA lead to an excitation-independent emission peak for N-CDs. Most importantly, N-CDs5000 can specifically stain nucleus, demonstrating specific targeting towards nucleus of BHK cells. These unique capabilities may be opens up the possibility of utilizing N-CDs5000 as fluorescent imaging agent for nucleus. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs5000 can be effectively quenched by Fe3+ ion and restored by ascorbic acid (AA). Therefore, N-CDs5000 can be a promising“on-off-on” probe for detection of Fe3+ and AA.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. J1103307) and Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21271094, 21771092) and Gansu Province Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 1508RJZA078). We also thank Professor Huichen Guo and Jiaxi Ru for his great efforts in the revised manuscript (State Key Laboratory of Veterinary Etiological Biology and Key Laboratory of Animal Virology of Ministry of Agriculture, Lanzhou Veterinary Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.11.175.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Nanoparticle-based immunochemical biosensors and assays: recent advances and challenges
Fluorescein-based chromo-fluorescent probe for zinc in aqueous solution: spirolactam ring opened or closed?

Recent advances in carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and biomedical applications

Insight into excitation-related luminescence properties of carbon dots: synergistic effect from photoluminescence centers in the carbon core and on the surface

Tuning optical properties and photocatalytic activities of carbon-based quantum dots through their surface groups

Multi sensing functions integrated into one carbon-dot based platform via different types of mechanisms

Tailor-made dual pH-sensitive polymer–doxorubicin nanoparticles for efficient anticancer drug delivery

Quaternized carbon dot-modified graphene oxide for selective cell labelling − controlled nucleus and cytoplasm imaging

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Bo Li received his Ph.D. degree from College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, PR China in 2017. He is currently a researcher in Qinghai Institute of Salt Lakes. His research interests focus on fluorescent chemosensors (CDs).

Deyan Gong received his B.S. degree from Xi’an University of Technology, PR China in2014. He is currently a master candidate at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, PR China. His research interests focus on developing fluorescent chemosensors.

Xinran Li received his MASTER degree from Lanzhou University Of Technology, PR China, in 2015. He is currently a Ph.D. scholar at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, PR China. His research interests focus on developing fluorescent chemosensors.
Lilan Zhang is currently a master candidate at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, PR China. Her research interests focus on developing fluorescent chemosensors.

Yaping Dong received her MASTER degree from Qinghai Institute of Salt Lakes, Chinese Academy of Sciences, in 1991. She is currently a researcher in Qinghai Institute of Salt Lakes. Her research interests focus on developing function materials.

Wu Li received his Ph.D. degree from Lanzhou University, PR China, in 1996. He is currently a researcher in Qinghai Institute of Salt Lakes. His research interests focus on developing function materials

Weisheng Liu received his Ph.D. degree from Lanzhou University, PR China, in 1996. He is currently a professor at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, PR China. His research interests focus on developing function materials and fluorescent chemosensors.

Wenwu Qin received his Ph.D. degree from Lanzhou University, PR China, in 2003.He is currently a professor at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, PR China. His research interests focus on developing fluorescent chemosensors.

Yabin Wang received his doctoral degree of Chemical Engineering and Technology from Harbin Institute of Technology in 2017. Now he works as a researcher in Yan’an University. His research interests focus on Surface and Interface Chemistry.
